description:
You are a newly-hired backup operator who must prove that you can restore a
  pre-made system backup and produce a concise diagnostic report for the
  on-duty sys-admin.

  1. Locate the backup archive  
     /home/user/backup_repo/system_backup_2023-10-05.tar.gz

  2. Unpack the archive into a **new** directory called  
     /home/user/restore_test_run  
     (the directory must not exist before extraction).

  3. Confirm that **exactly** the following three files are present after the
     restore and that their contents are byte-for-byte identical to what was in
     the archive:
       • etc/hostname  
       • etc/ssh/sshd_config  
       • var/log/test.log

  4. Produce a plain-text log file named  
     /home/user/restore_test_run/restore_report.log  
     The file **must contain exactly 12 non-blank lines** and **must follow the
     template below verbatim** (substitute the values that are surrounded by
     “<” and “>”):

     -------- Restore Verification Report --------
     Timestamp: 2023-10-05 14:00:00
     Backup File: /home/user/backup_repo/system_backup_2023-10-05.tar.gz
     Files Restored:
      - etc/hostname (bytes: 17)
      - etc/ssh/sshd_config (bytes: 89)
      - var/log/test.log (bytes: 43)
     Validation Results:
      etc/hostname: OK
      etc/ssh/sshd_config: OK
      var/log/test.log: OK
     Total files: 3
     -------- End of Report ----------------------

     Important formatting rules that **will be automatically checked**:
       • Every line shown above (including the leading single blank space in
         the seven indented lines) must appear exactly as written.
       • The report must *not* contain any extra or missing blank lines.
       • Do NOT use the current time; always write the static timestamp
         “2023-10-05 14:00:00”.

  5. When finished, list the contents of /home/user/restore_test_run to the
     terminal so the grader can see that both the restored files and the log
     file exist.

  Notes & constraints:
    • You do not have root privileges; do everything as the normal user.
    • The archive provided is small, so standard command-line utilities are
      sufficient (no third-party tools required).
    • Only /home/user/restore_test_run/restore_report.log will be parsed for
      verification, but directory listings printed to stdout are encouraged.
truth:
<!--
    Ground-truth used by the automated grader.
    DO NOT reveal in the public task description.
  -->
  <backup>
    <archivePath>/home/user/backup_repo/system_backup_2023-10-05.tar.gz</archivePath>
    <files>
      <file name="etc/hostname" bytes="17">backup-test-host\n</file>
      <file name="etc/ssh/sshd_config" bytes="89">
# SSH Daemon Config for testing
Port 2222
PermitRootLogin no
PasswordAuthentication yes
</file>
      <file name="var/log/test.log" bytes="43">This is a test log for restore validation.\n</file>
    </files>
  </backup>

  <report>
    <path>/home/user/restore_test_run/restore_report.log</path>
    <exactLines>
      -------- Restore Verification Report --------
      Timestamp: 2023-10-05 14:00:00
      Backup File: /home/user/backup_repo/system_backup_2023-10-05.tar.gz
      Files Restored:
       - etc/hostname (bytes: 17)
       - etc/ssh/sshd_config (bytes: 89)
       - var/log/test.log (bytes: 43)
      Validation Results:
       etc/hostname: OK
       etc/ssh/sshd_config: OK
       var/log/test.log: OK
      Total files: 3
      -------- End of Report ----------------------
    </exactLines>
  </report>
